result:
I want to write about the moment you realize the story you’ve been telling yourself about your own life is only one of several equally true drafts, and that the margins are wider than you thought, and that the paper itself is edible, flammable, dissolvable, recyclable, and sometimes already folded into the shape of a boat or a bird without your noticing.  
I want to write about the smell of photocopier toner at 3:14 a.m. in a university library basement, how it hangs in the air like the ghost of every unfinished term paper, how it combines with the faint sweetness of the vending-machine mocha that costs two dollars and explodes into chemical approximation of hazelnut when you puncture the foil lid.  
I want to write about the girl who once used that photocopier to copy her own hands at random, page after page, because she had read that if you pressed your palm to the glass hard enough the machine would capture the lifeline, the heartline, the scar you got from a lemonade jar in 1997, and someday, long after checkout cards were obsolete, someone would find those phantom hands in the recycle bin and feel less alone.  
I want to write about the man who changed his name to a color no one can pronounce, a color he claims exists only in the ultraviolet spectrum of bee vision, a color he swears tastes like forgiveness, and how he insists we’ve all spoken this color in dreams but forget it the instant the alarm clock coughs, and how he carries around a tiny vial of sugar water labeled with that unpronounceable name as if it were a vaccine against despair.  
I want to write about the year I tried to become nocturnal on purpose, removing every bulb from my apartment, taping blackout foil to the windows, reversing the day into a bat-winged negative so that groceries were purchased at 5 a.m. dew-point, friends became disembodied voices on delayed voice notes, and the sun itself felt like a rude houseguest who arrived just as I was finally clean and dream-stitched.  
I want to write about the first time I understood that my mother had once been a teenager who sneaked out to smoke mint cigarettes behind the Piggly Wiggly, that her first kiss tasted like chlorine and fear, that the reason she hated the smell of burnt matches was because they reminded her of a boy who promised write but sent only a single Polaroid of his own foot in a cowboy boot.  
I want to write about the last time I saw my grandfather alive, how he mistook me for his brother who died at Anzio, how he told me—eyes bright with morphine and something kinder than time—to hide the canned peaches from the Germans, and how when I promised I would, he patted my cheek with a hand whose veins were blue roadmaps to every place he’d never revisit, and how that was the closest we ever came to saying I love you in a language neither of us had to translate.  
I want to write about the science of phantom-traffic jams, how a single brake tap propagates backward like a soliton wave until miles of brake lights stutter red Morse across the dusk, and how sometimes I think grief works the same way: someone centuries ago touched the glass, and we’re still idling, engine overheating, mixtape warping in the dashboard.  
I want to write about the mixtape, the actual TDK SA-90 I hid inside the drop ceiling of my high-school darkroom in 1998, Side A labeled “For the girl who develops film like she’s defusing a bomb,” Side B label left blank because I ran out of courage. I used a Radio Shack gender-bender to record directly from CD to cassette, violating every copyright prayer in the Continental United States; the last song is “Thunder Road” cut off at 3:47 because the tape ran out mid-harmonica, which felt like the exact acoustic shape of almost.  
I want to write about the word almost, how it smells like rain on hot asphalt, how it carries a tiny suitcase of miniature sunsets, how it is the patron saint of motel neon and half-finished bridges and the second before the roller-coaster crests, how it is both vaccine and virus, how we carry it in our wallet like a photo of a house we almost bought, how sometimes late at night we take it out and crease it along the roofline just to remember the floor plan of a life we might have lived.  
I want to write about the floor plan of the house I drew when I was seven, a house shaped like an ampersand, with a swimming pool in the loop and a library in the tail, and how my father looked at it and said, “That’s not structurally sound,” and how I decided then that structural soundness was overrated, that I would rather live in conjunction than in foundation, that I would rather be a hallway that leads to another hallway forever than a room that ends in a wall.  
I want to write about the wall that fell in the 1989 earthquake, how it revealed the skeleton of a 1920s swimming suit inside the lathe, wool still indigo where the sun never reached, as if someone had undressed mid-construction and walked into the future naked, and how the newspapers used the phrase “time capsule” but I kept thinking “escape hatch,” and how I stole a button from that suit and still carry it like a compass that points only backward.  
I want to write about backward telescopes, about watching your childhood shrink to a blue marble, then to a grain of rice, then to a single pixel you can cover with the tip of a finger, and how the finger still smells like cafeteria pizza and chlorine and the purple ditto ink we licked because someone said it tasted like grape, and how it never did, it tasted like permission.  
I want to write about permission, the unsigned permission slip for the field trip to the planetarium that I forged in the parking lot with a broken Bic, how Jupiter looked like a cauliflower when it finally rolled across the dome, how the girl beside me whispered that the rings of Saturn sounded like her father’s laugh on long-distance calls from Saudi Arabia, how I wanted to invent a new constellation called The Unreturned Phone Call right there in the fake-night sky.  
I want to write about the fake-night sky installed in the casino ceiling in Vegas, how the Big Dipper is backwards, how the air is pumped with extra oxygen and the scent of coconut so you never know what time it is, how once at 4 a.m. I watched an Elvis impersonator cry into a bowl of pho at the food court, how his sideburns were slipping off like surrender flags, how he told me the cure for heartbreak is to sing “Love Me Tender” in the key of the plumbing until security escorts you out.  
I want to write about plumbing, the hidden rivers inside every wall, how they carry away our daily sins—toothpaste, antifreeze, the microplastics of exfoliated hope—how they gurgle a language older than any alphabet, how if you press your ear to the bath drain at the exact instant the neighbor upstairs flushes, you can hear a cathedral of waterfalls inside a pipe only four inches wide.  
I want to write about four inches, the distance between the hotel room door and the corridor carpet where I once set down a tray of chicken wings so I could swipe the key card without dropping the plastic bucket of ice, how in those four inches I understood that adulthood is mostly a balancing act conducted in strangers’ hallways while wearing mismatched socks, how the ice was already halfway to becoming the water it used to be, how the wings were Buffalo but tasted like the approximate ZIP code of home.  
I want to write about ZIP codes, how they are accidental poems—90210 the haiku of fourteen-year-old longing, 99723 the sestina of permafrost and pipeline, 70116 the sonnet of second-line parades where the tubas polish the air until it shines like brass, how I once mailed a postcard to my own address but wrote only “I saw a pelican today and thought of you” and how three days later it arrived scrubbed by sorting machines, canceled with a wavy black line that looked like a tiny EKG.  
I want to write about EKGs, the time I watched my own heart plotted in jagged neon inside an ambulance and how the paramedic said, “That’s a good rhythm, keep it up,” as if the heart were a nightclub act, and how I wanted to ask him what happens to the flat lines on the screen after they vanish, whether they go to a valley of quiet horizon, whether they get a second chance at percussion.  
I want to write about percussion, the drum circle that forms every Sunday dusk on the concrete pier where the cruise ships vomit passengers back into the city, how the conga skins are patched with duct tape and hope, how a kid with one chopstick and an overturned bucket keeps perfect 7/8 time, how the seagulls orbit like cymbals waiting for their crash, how the sunset conducts with a baton of pure copper, and how every beat is a small forgiveness for the week we almost survived.  
I want to write about survival, the kind that looks like buying a single lime at the grocery store because it is the cheapest green thing you can own, how you set it on the windowsill like a paperweight for the wind, how weeks later it shrivels into a golf ball of sincerity, and you finally cut it open and the inside is still a sunrise, and you squeeze that last teaspoon into a glass of tap water and drink it like a contract with continuation.  
I want to write about continuation, the way the sentence refuses to end even when the grammar police wave their truncheons, the way the paragraph keeps inhaling, the way the scroll bar shrinks to a rice grain, the way the reader’s eyes keep moving even when the coffee is cold, because somewhere inside the alphabet is a lighthouse powered entirely by the willingness to keep going, and the light from that tower is made of every story we ever swallowed instead of told.  
I want to write about swallowing, the time I kept a pebble from the shore of every sea I ever visited, how I stored them in an Altoids tin until the hinges broke, how one night I lined them up on the table like a necklace for a giant, how the smallest one looked exactly like the moon seen from the wrong side, how I swallowed that one by accident during a sneeze and waited for tides to form in my blood, and how nothing happened except a faint sound of surf when I hiccuped, which could have been imagination or could have been proof that we contain more coastline than we think.  
I want to write about proof, the mathematical kind that lives on napkins in jazz bars, where the trumpeter scribbles a lemma between sets and insists it proves the existence of yesterday, how the napkin gets soaked in ring-shaped condensation so the ink blooms into galaxies, how the bartender sweeps it into the trash without asking for citations, and how somewhere in a landfill that theorem is still decomposing, teaching the seagulls that time is non-commutative.  
I want to write about non-commutativity, how the order of kisses matters, how kiss A then B does not equal kiss B then A if the moon has moved even a finger-width between them, how memory keeps the receipt but not the currency, how we keep trying to balance the equation by adding more mouth, more tongue, more apology, but the variables keep reproducing like fruit flies in the compost bin of almost.  
I want to write about compost, the moral imperative of rot, the way last week’s lettuce becomes next month’s tomatoes, the way the worms write cursive in the dark, the way the steam rising from the pile on a cold morning is the breath of a thousand tiny dragons converting your banana peels into belief, and how if you stand close enough you can hear them whisper the word humus which is only one letter away from human, which is only one letter away from humor, which is only one letter away from tumor, and how all four live inside us at once.  
I want to write about tumors, the benign one my dog had the size of a golf ball under his chin, how it wobbled when he barked like a second head that had forgotten language, how the vet removed it while I held his paw and whispered every synonym for stay, how the incision looked like a zipper to a country I couldn’t visit, how he woke up wagging as if pockets could be unsewn and still hold everything important.  
I want to write about unsewn pockets, the pair of jeans I cut into shorts at seventeen, how the fray revealed a folded note from the factory worker in Guangzhou who wrote in ballpoint “may you find what you’re looking for,” how I carried that note through three laundries until the ink disappeared, how I like to believe the words soaked into my skin and are still circling, waiting for the right bruise to surface through.  
I want to write about surfaces, the way the ocean is a Möbius strip of horizon, the way the word “horizon” contains “zion” which contains “on” which contains “o” which is the shape of every mouth that ever watched a ship disappear and hoped it was only repositioning, not ending, the way the tide keeps licking the sand like a cat that forgets the bowl is full, the way every seventh wave is supposedly bigger but nobody counts past grief.  
I want to write about counting, the childhood ritual of seconds after lightning before thunder, how my mother taught me to divide by five to approximate miles, how I still do it at thirty-eight even when the storm is inside the apartment, even when the flash is the bulb of the fridge opening at 2 a.m. and the crash is the leftover meatloaf sliding to the floor, and how I stand there barefoot doing math in the dark like a good student of weather.  
I want to write about students, the adult literacy class I tutored in the basement of the Methodist church, how Carlos at sixty-five learned to spell “daughter” and then cried because his had died at nine from asthma, how the word looked too small on the chalkboard to hold a person, how we added more letters—d-a-u-g-h-t-e-r-∞—until the infinity sign looked like a rope thrown across the paper, and how he kept that paper folded in his shirt pocket like a wing.  
I want to write about wings, the pigeon born without one who lived on the fire escape outside my office, how it learned to hop in spirals, how the others treated it like a guru of partial flight, how one day I found it gone and assumed the worst until I saw it on the adjacent roof two stories higher, having hopped through railings and air and rumor, and how ever since I measure progress not by altitude but by the audacity to keep redefining ground.  
I want to write about ground, the earthquake drill in first grade where we knelt under our tiny desks, how I kissed the terrazzo because I thought it might be the last thing I ever touched, how the taste was dust and cafeteria ammonia and the ghost of milk, how I waited for the world to crack open and reveal the candy center, and how when nothing happened I felt mildly cheated, and how that was my first lesson in catastrophe as postponement.  
I want to write about postponement, the way deadlines bloom like mushrooms overnight, the way the email starts “Sorry for the delay” as if time were a train we keep missing, the way the unfinished novel lives in a folder called “Later” which has become a universe expanding silently inside the laptop, colonizing gigabytes with characters who age in real time even when the document is closed, and how sometimes at 3 a.m. one of them knocks from the inside of the screen and I apologize like a landlord with no vacancy.  
I want to write about vacancy, the neon sign outside the motel that reads VACANCY but the A is flickering so it says V Cancy, as if inviting us to insert whatever we’ve lost—virginity, velocity, vagrancy, vaccine, valediction—and how the clerk behind the bullet-proof glass keeps a list of words people suggest, taped to the wall like a prayer wheel for the alphabet’s missing teeth.  
I want to write about teeth, the wisdom tooth I kept after extraction, how it looks like a miniature iceberg, how I carried it through four moves in a film canister, how it rolls out sometimes when I’m looking for quarters and I hold it like a tiny hourglass that measures nothing, and how I can’t throw it away because it once knew the inside of me before I knew the outside of anything.  
I want to write about anything, the promise embedded in that word, how it opens the sentence like a parachute made of maybe, how it guarantees continuation beyond the horizon of the period, how it is the patron saint of overflow, how it says: keep going, the page is edible, the ink is drinkable, the story is still a rumor in the throat of the next syllable, and we are all just vowels learning to hold hands across the consonants of distance.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
